data.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T3 (Integration feasibility) [overlay].

NOTE ON TARGET: the chunk under review is the **workflow-engine remediation epic** ("unify LLM functionality on the engine, restore visual editing, document intended use"), not the plan-review gate epic itself. Posting here as the linked/parent context; the finding + affirmations concern that remediation epic's WS-VISUAL / WS-VALIDATE / WS-MIGRATE workstreams.

APPLICABILITY: T3 partially APPLIES. Several workstreams contemplate adopting/relying on EXTERNAL technologies the engine has not used before — WS-VISUAL (BPMN/bpmn.io/Camunda, n8n, Node-RED, Argo, draw.io, Mermaid round-trip), WS-VALIDATE (Langflow, Burr, Temporal/Prefect/Dagster, LangGraph, Argo) — so the overlay engages for those. By contrast WS-VISUAL-EVAL's Langfuse/Inspect/promptfoo surfaces are INTERNAL, already-used integrations (tool-verified: src/rebar/llm/tracing.py + config.LangfuseConfig; the [eval] extra wires inspect_ai + promptfoo JUnit interop at src/rebar/llm/eval.py:281-289; src/rebar/_optional.py:29) → T3 not-applicable there per the ANTI-FP "internal, already-used integration" rule.

AFFIRMATIONS (T3 checks this epic passes):
- T3(a) technical_feasibility / capability-gap NAMED: the epic names its central capability gap honestly and grounds it correctly — WS-MIGRATE states "the engine is a thin LINEAR executor (graphlib) — the gate's dynamic fan-out (Pass-1 → N findings → N Pass-2 verifier calls) + registry-driven facet-chunking may EXCEED the DSL." Tool-verified accurate: the executor runs a single linear topological pass (src/rebar/llm/workflow/executor.py:457 `graphlib.TopologicalSorter(...).static_order()`; interpreter.py:276 same), so the asserted gap is real, not speculative. The plan then routes the open question forward ("resolve whether the DSL gains map/fan-out steps or the orchestration stays scripted inside steps") rather than asserting an unproven capability.
- T3(b) no asserted-but-false external subcommand/endpoint: WS-VISUAL/WS-VALIDATE list candidate TECHNOLOGIES to weigh, explicitly "NOT decided" / "Candidates to weigh in the brainstorm" — they do not assert that a specific BPMN/n8n/Argo subcommand or endpoint exists, so there is no MATCH/MISMATCH to verify and nothing to flag as a false capability claim. Appropriately UNVERIFIED-by-design at the epic stage.
- T3(d) critical gap routes BEFORE committing: the epic's whole posture is "this epic CAPTURES scope + initial concerns; a dedicated brainstorm session does the deep design BEFORE implementation (per joe-brainstorm)" and "Scope (workstreams — decomposed AFTER the brainstorm)." That is exactly the spike-before-commit discipline T3(d) asks for — the external-tech decision is explicitly held open and not committed in this epic.
- T3(c) auth/HTTPS: the candidate visual-editing tools are local desktop/round-trip editors over text files (git-clean-diff constraint), not networked services requiring an auth/HTTPS precondition; none asserted, none needed at this stage.

FINDING (one coaching item):

- [ ] Pin a feasibility SPIKE/proof-of-round-trip GATE on the WS-VISUAL format/technology decision BEFORE any downstream child commits to it — the load-bearing external capability is "an established ecosystem provides BIDIRECTIONAL visual editing AND the format preserves clean git diffs + schema-validated text," and that round-trip property is an asserted-but-currently-UNVERIFIED capability of every candidate (BPMN-XML, n8n/Node-RED JSON, draw.io, Mermaid round-trip). T3 rates an asserted-but-unverified external capability a plan depends on as MAJOR; the epic states the CONSTRAINT ("preserve clean git diffs + schema-validated text — the a88f invariant") but does not make "verify the chosen tool actually round-trips edits back to clean, schema-valid, diff-friendly text" an explicit gate before adoption. Add to WS-VISUAL an acceptance step that the brainstorm's chosen format must DEMONSTRATE (small spike) a human-edit → save → clean-git-diff → schema-validate round-trip before it is adopted, so a downstream story cannot commit to a format whose bidirectional/diff claim was never proven.
  - criteria: T3
  - location: epic §Scope → WS-VISUAL ("Decide a workflow format/technology whose ecosystem ALREADY provides visual (ideally bidirectional) editing … CONSTRAINT: preserve clean git diffs + schema-validated text"); related WS-VALIDATE ("Output: a kept-or-pivot recommendation")
  - evidence:
    - WS-VISUAL asserts a dependency on an external ecosystem providing bidirectional visual editing but lists only undecided candidates and states the git-diff/schema constraint as a CONSTRAINT, not as a verify-before-commit gate.
    - The bidirectional-round-trip-with-clean-diffs property is tool/format-specific and not derivable from the candidate names alone (e.g. BPMN-XML and n8n JSON differ sharply in diff-friendliness and schema-validatability) → it is precisely the UNVERIFIED external capability T3 targets.
    - The a88f invariant the plan must preserve (git-native, schema-validated workflow text) makes the round-trip the make-or-break feasibility question, so it warrants an explicit spike gate rather than a downstream assumption.
  - suggested_fix: In WS-VISUAL, add an acceptance line: "Before adopting any format, run a thin SPIKE proving a human-visual-edit → write-back → `git diff` (clean/minimal) → schema-validate round-trip on a representative workflow; a format that fails this is rejected, and the kept-or-pivot recommendation (WS-VALIDATE) records the spike result." Leave WS-VALIDATE's alternatives re-eval as-is (it already outputs a kept-or-pivot recommendation, which is the right shape).

Net: T3 is mostly satisfied by the epic's explicit brainstorm-before-commit posture and its honest, code-accurate naming of the linear-executor capability gap; the single coaching item is to make the external visual-format's round-trip feasibility an explicit spike gate rather than an un-pinned downstream assumption.
